summary: McKenzie provides a brief accounting update on his Bingo Card Creator side project, noting he's spent about 10 hours and $43 total since launch. He describes two free marketing channels he discovered: PAD files for automatic software directory listings, and teacher bulletin boards from the mid-90s that accept commercial software posts. The bulletin board strategy yielded high-PageRank inbound links and actual customer inquiries. He reflects with surprise that honest, clearly-labeled advertising actually generates responses.
key_points: Total project expenditure remains remarkably low at $43.22 across Google, Yahoo, and eBay advertising experiments
PAD files provide free, automated exposure across software download sites with minimal effort — a high-leverage tactic for shareware developers
Niche community bulletin boards from the mid-90s offer free posting opportunities with high PageRank backlinks and real customer engagement
Honest, straightforward advertising in the right context can outperform expectations — people do read ads when they're properly classified
money_quotes: Since launch I've probably spent ~10 hours (some of them at work — sorry boss!) obsessing over the project and doing various tweaks here and there.
That was a great deal of exposure for not a penny and not a lot of effort.
I highly recommend anybody who writes consumer-oriented shareware to give this a whirl.
Completely on the up and up and got me several nice inbound links from sites with obscene PRs (a handful of 7s and a bag full of 6s).
People actually read advertisements when they are classified as advertisements? That amazes me, but it really shouldn't seeing that I'm paying for folks to do it every day.
Its heartening to know you can still do well for yourself by being honest with folks.
key_insight: Free, honest promotion in niche communities where your target audience already gathers can yield disproportionate returns in both SEO value and actual customer interest.
